mnemonics.title: CRITICAL UPkeep
mnemonics.content: C-orrectness, R-eliability, I-ntegrity, T-estability, A-vailability, L-Usability, U-tility, M-aintainability, P-ortability - McCall's quality factors

mnemonics.title: My Coffee Pot Looks Extremely Hot
mnemonics.content: M-CPU, LOC, EF, HT - Halstead's metrics: (M)ain program length, (C)omputed program length, (P)rogram volume, (U)gram difficulty, (L)anguage level, (O)perators, (E)xpected bugs, (H)alstead's equation

mnemonics.title: PQRST For Quality Assessment
mnemonics.content: P-roduct operation, Q-uality characteristics, R-evision capabilities, S-pecial attributes, T-ransition - ISO/IEC 25010 quality model

mnemonics.title: Don't Take Medicine Twice Daily
mnemonics.content: Defect metrics: D-efect density, T-ime to repair, M-TBF (Mean Time Between Failures), T-est effectiveness

acronyms.usage: Basic size metric; higher LOC in complex modules indicates more testing needed
Measures software functionality independent of programming language; used for effort estimation
Measures code complexity based on decision points; CC > 10 indicates high-risk code needing refactoring
Reliability metric; higher MTBF means more reliable system
Maintainability metric; lower MTTR indicates easier maintenance
Defects per KLOC; industry standard: <1 defects/KLOC is excellent, >5 is poor
Estimates effort and schedule based on KLOC and development mode (organic, semi-detached, embedded)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a maze with multiple branching paths. Each decision point (if, while, for, case) adds a new path. Count the paths to determine complexity. A straight line = complexity 1, one branch = 2, etc.

Think of a 3-story building: Bottom floor = Product Operation (correctness, reliability, efficiency), Middle floor = Product Revision (maintainability, flexibility, testability), Top floor = Product Transition (portability, reusability, interoperability)
Picture a city map divided into blocks (KLOC). Each defect is a red dot. A crowded red map = high defect density (dangerous area), sparse dots = quality code
Imagine 5 types of boxes: External Inputs, External Outputs, External Inquiries, Internal Logical Files, External Interface Files. Count and weigh each box type to calculate FP

analogies.analogy: Like a doctor's medical checkup: Just as blood pressure, heart rate, and BMI tell us about human health, metrics like LOC, cyclomatic complexity, and defect density tell us about software health
Like counting doors in a house to escape: More doors (decision points) = more escape routes but also more confusing layout. A simple house has 1-2 doors, complex mansions have many
Like a car's reliability: A car that breaks down every 2 months has low MTBF; a reliable car that runs for 5 years without issues has high MTBF
Like crime rate per neighborhood: A safe area has 1 crime per 1000 residents (like <1 defect/KLOC), a dangerous area has 10+ crimes per 1000 (like high defect density)

Like counting rooms in a house for valuation: A house is valued by number and size of rooms (functions), not by how many bricks or nails used (lines of code). Same applies to software - functionality matters, not code length

Like a modular kitchen: Well-organized kitchen with separate sections for cooking, storage, washing (high maintainability) vs. cramped single-corner kitchen where everything is mixed (low maintainability)
